coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots as nanocatalytic medicine for anti-inflammation therapy
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Kong, Bo
Yang, Tong
Cheng, Feng
Qian, Yan
Li, Chunmei
Zhan, Lei
Li, Yuanfang
Zou, Hongyan
Huang, Chengzhi
coredata.dc:description:
Aberrant reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation is one of the crucial mediators in the pathogenesis of inflammation. So, the development of nanocatalytic medicine to catalyze the ROS-scavenging reactions in pathological regions are promising for anti-inflammatory therapy. Herein, a type of biocompatible metal free carbon dots is prepared via a hydrothermal method which can exhibit peroxidase (POD)-like, catalase (CAT)-like and superoxide dismutase (SOD)-like activities. It has been found that the carbon dots have the capability to efficiently deplete the excessive ROS such as peroxide (H2O2), superoxide anion (O2
−) and hydroxyl radical (
OH) for their abundant functional groups. After the tail injection in mice with liver inflammation induced by lipopolysaccharide, the carbon dots efficiently reduced the excessive production of ROS and proinflammatory cytokines in vitro. Both in vitro and in vivo results endowed the biocompatible carbon dots with great potential in nanocatalytic medicine for the treatment of disease.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Multienzyme
Reactive oxygen species scavenging
Catalytic medicine
Anti-inflammation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Chemical titration of oxygen functional groups on CDs
Multienzyme-like activities of CDs and ROS depletion
Anti-inflammatory effect of CDs in vitro and in vivo

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Natural Science Foundation of Chongqing, China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This study was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC, Grant No. 21976144 , 21535006 , and 21705130 ), Chongqing Talents Program for Outstanding Scientists (Grant No.cstc2021ycjh-bgzxm0178) and Natural Science Foundation of Chongqing, China (Grant No. cstc2020jcyj-msxmX0873).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of the as-prepared CDs. (A) TEM image (Insets: the size distributions of CDs obtained using the statistical analysis of 100 nanoparticles. Scale bar are equal to 200 nm, 50 nm and 2 nm, respectively). (B) The infrared spectra of the as-prepared CDs. Full-scan XPS profiles of the CDs (C) and high resolution spectra for C 1 s (D), N 1 s (E), and O 1 s (F) with peak deconvolution.

CDs had antioxidant enzymatic activities of POD (A, B), CAT (C, D), SOD (E) and that of scavenging
OH (F). (A) The POD-like activity of CDs as identified by TMB. (B) The mechanism of CDs acting as POD. (C) Photographs of CAT systems reacting for 5 min at different pH. CDs, 0.6 mg/mL; H2O2, 0.3 M; BR buffer solution. (D) Oxygen generation catalyzed by CDs which got increased gradually with time at pH 2.0, 5.0, 7.4, and 11.4. (E) Change in DMPO/
OOH ESR signal upon addition of CDs at pH 7.4. (F) The effect of CDs on
OH from Fenton reagents at pH 3.6. Change in BMPO/
OH ESR signal upon addition of CDs. Data represented mean ± SD (n = 3).

Decrease of the ROS of RAW264.7 cells by CDs. (A) CDs inhibited ROS generation induced by RAW264.7 cells. (a) Control: DCFH-DA; (b) 5 μg/mL CDs/DCFH-DA; (c) 15 μg/mL CDs/DCFH-DA; Fluorescence microscopic images (a − c), bright-field microscopic images (d − f), and merged images (g − i) of RAW264.7 cells. Scale bar, 30 μm. (B) CDs inhibited ROS generation induced by LPS. (a) Control: DCFH-DA; (b) 1 μg/mL LPS/DCFH-DA; (c) 5 μg/mL CDs/1 μg/mL LPS/DCFH-DA; (d) 15 μg/mL CDs/1 μg/mL LPS/DCFH-DA; Fluorescence microscopic images (a − d), bright-field microscopic images (e − h), and merged images (i − l) of RAW264.7 cells. Scale bar, 30 μm. (C) Cell supernatant sample for TNF-α of control group, LPS-treated mice group and LPS-treated group with incubation of CDs. ### indicates p < 0.001 as compared to control group; *** indicates p < 0.001 as compared to LPS group. (D) Western Blot analysis for iNOS and COX2 in different experimental groups. (E) LPS were used as stimulators of the inflammation models. RAW264.7 cells were pretreated with the indicated concentrations of CDs (0, 2, 4, 6, 8, and 10 μg/mL) for 24 h, and then the medium was replaced into medium containing 1 μg/mL LPS and incubated for 24 h, respectively. Data represented mean ± SD (n = 5).

CDs protected mice livers from LPS-induced inflammation. (A) Ex vivo images of isolated organs of mice after injection of CDs (20 mg/kg) at different times. (B) FL intensity of the CDs from isolated organs at different times after injection of CDs (20 mg/kg). (C) The pathological morphology and ROS intensities in mice liver tissues. (D) Hematoxylin and Eosin (H&E) staining images of liver histology. Liver tissues were excised from (a) control, (b) LPS-treated mice, and LPS-treated mice with 1 week preinjection of different concentration of CDs (c) 5 mg/kg (d) 20 mg/kg. TUNEL staining images of mice liver tissue sections from (e) control, (f) LPS-treated mice, and LPS-treated mice with 1 week preinjection of different concentration of CDs (g) 5 mg/kg (h) 20 mg/kg. Data represented mean ± SD (n = 5).
Schematic illustration of CDs for ROS depletion and anti-inflammation therapy.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots as nanocatalytic medicine for anti-inflammation therapy

College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Chongqing Key Laboratory of Luminescent and Real-Time Analysis System, Chongqing Science and Technology Commission, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Beibei, Chongqing 400715, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Luminescence Analysis and Molecular Sensing (Southwest University), Ministry of Education

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Metal free carbon dots (CDs) are developed to act as the antioxidant multienzyme, which directly deplete the excessive ROS including H2O2, O2
−, and
OH and effectively alleviate the inflammation of inflamed liver tissues through intrinsic passive targeting capability, endowing them with a high promise in nanocatalytic medicine for anti-inflammation therapy.

Aberrant reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation is one of the crucial mediators in the pathogenesis of inflammation. So, the development of nanocatalytic medicine to catalyze the ROS-scavenging reactions in pathological regions are promising for anti-inflammatory therapy. Herein, a type of biocompatible metal free carbon dots is prepared via a hydrothermal method which can exhibit peroxidase (POD)-like, catalase (CAT)-like and superoxide dismutase (SOD)-like activities. It has been found that the carbon dots have the capability to efficiently deplete the excessive ROS such as peroxide (H2O2), superoxide anion (O2
−) and hydroxyl radical (
OH) for their abundant functional groups. After the tail injection in mice with liver inflammation induced by lipopolysaccharide, the carbon dots efficiently reduced the excessive production of ROS and proinflammatory cytokines in vitro. Both in vitro and in vivo results endowed the biocompatible carbon dots with great potential in nanocatalytic medicine for the treatment of disease.

ce:section.ce:para:
Reactive oxygen species (ROS), including hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), superoxide anion (O2
−), hydroxyl radical (
OH), and singlet oxygen (1O2), participate in many biological activities and play important roles in regulating human physiological functions [1–4]. However, overexpressed ROS can induce the oxidative stress with pathobiological consequences [5–7]. Abnormal production of ROS has been elucidated as the key mediators in the pathogenesis of inflammation, a complicated physiological response phenomenon to injury, infection, and stress [8,9]. The excessive ROS can cause the release of calcium ions, which further promotes the production of ROS and aggravates inflammation [10]. Therefore, to scavenge the overproduced ROS in living organisms is the significant issue in the treatment of inflammatory [11,12].

Many chemical strategies have been developed in the past decade to scavenge excessive ROS and relieve inflammation, among which emerging nanocatalytic therapeutic modality using catalytically active nanomaterials for scavenging high‐toxic ROS have attracted more and more attention [13,14]. These overproduced ROS in vivo are usually involved in catalytic chemical reactions and thus establish a profound basis for inflammatory-specific catalytic therapy [15]. For the excessive accumulation of ROS, the strong oxidizing microenvironment usually appears in the inflammatory tissue, thus, the catalysts should be designed as the antioxidants, which can decrease ROS in pathological areas. Recently, a lot of ROS-scavenging nanomaterials with antioxidant enzymatic functions have been developed to down regulate aberrant ROS state since they have high antioxidant catalytic activities like enzyme, such as catalase (CAT), superoxide dismutase (SOD) and peroxidase (POD) reductase, et al [16–20]. Prussian blue nanoparticles, for instance, display multienzyme-like properties including POD, CAT, and SOD and could effectively decrease intracellular ROS level, achieving excellent cytoprotection efficiency [21]. CeO2 nanoparticles, one of the most prevalent inorganic nanozyme, present SOD and CAT activities owing to their mixed valence states of cerium (III) and (IV), scavenging O2
− and H2O2 efficiently and being potentially applied for anti-inflammatory applications [22–25]. Mn3O4 nanoparticles, possessing POD and CAT mimicking activities and hydroxyl radicals scavenging capacity, can treat the ear-inflammation induced by the ROS [26]. All these researches indicate that developing multi-enzyme mimicking materials to scavenge multiple ROS simultaneously promise their potential applicability in nanocatalytic medicine [15,27]. Most of antioxidative nanomaterials were developed based on the variable valence state of metal centers [28,29]. However, these metal-containing nanomaterials usually pose some safety concerns for their unwanted metal ion leakage and possible residues. Metal-free nanomaterials such as polydopamine and melanin nanoparticles have been used as efficient ROS scavengers [24,30]. However, the unclear polymerization and degradation mechanism may induce some application barriers such as in vivo instability, the uncertain transport route and potential toxicity to human body. So, it still remains challenging to develop new catalytic nanoagents with efficient ROS scavenging capacity and negligible toxicity.”

Carbon nanomaterials, such as carbon nanodots (CDs), have attracted great research interest in biomedicine owing to their distinctive advantages, such as good biocompatibility, photostability and inherent targeting ability for their functional groups [20,31–34]. Since the abundant functional groups around the sp2-hybrided carbon core in their core–shell structure, CDs display enzymatic catalysis properties that can be selectively activated [35]. Chiral carbon dots have reported to mimic topoisomerase I to mediate the topological rearrangement of supercoiled DNA enantioselectivity [36]. Since the CDs are usually prepared using small organic molecules as precursors, and are free of metal ions active catalytic site. It has been reported that the CDs display peroxidase (POD)-like activity through the catalytic sites of CO groups [37]. Then, a type of selenium-doped CDs have been reported to have POD mimicking activity and can effectively scavenge
OH [38]. Bi et al developed a self-healing hydrogel for the reloadable delivery of carbon quantum dots (C-dots) for reactive oxygen species (ROS) removal at an inflammation site [28]. Prof. Qu’s group prepared a novel oxygenated groups enriched graphene quantum dots as highly efficient enzyme mimics and furtherly presented functionalized graphene quantum dots with preferential renal accumulation as high-efficacy ROS scavengers for acute kidney injury treatment [39]. It is herein conceived that the good catalytic performance of the CDs would enable promising application potential. Although many breakthroughs have occurred in the synthesis and applications of CDs, the nanocataltical applications of CDs in nanomedical fields are still in their infancy.

Herein, we report a new metal free carbon dots (CDs) prepared through a hydrothermal route with the precursors p-phenylenediamine and ethylenediamine. Rich functional groups on the surface as the efficient electron transporters endow the CDs exhibiting peroxidase (POD)-like, catalase (CAT)-like and superoxide dismutase (SOD)-like activities. What’s more, the newly developed CDs show intrinsic catalytic ROS-scavenging capabilities, which are passively targeted and accumulated toward inflamed liver tissues, leading to the good therapeutic efficacies in murine models of inflammatory liver diseases (Scheme 1 ).

All chemicals used were of high-quality analytical grade. Ethylenediamine, p-Phenylenediamine, 3,3′,5,5′-Tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), 2-bromo-1-phenylethanone (BrPE), benzoic anhydride (BA) and phenylhydrazine (PH) have been purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Lipopolysaccharide (LPS) (L2630) and 2′,7′-dichlorodihydrofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (USA). Cell-counting kit-8 (CCK-8) was obtained from Dojindo Laboratories. Mouse TNF-α ELISA Kit (Mouse Tumor Necrosis Factor-α Enzyme-Linked ImmunoSorbent Assay Kit) and Lyso-Tracker Red were purchased from Beyotime Institute of Biotechnology (China).

0.11 g p-phenylenediamine, 535 μL ethylenediamine and 3.0 mL anhydrous ethanol were mixed in a 25 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave, then heated for 12 h at 180 °C. And a brown product solution was finally obtained. The as-prepared CDs were acted as multifunctional carbon nanozyme. During the experiments, different batches of the CDs were prepared in the same way.

TMB was used as substrates to determine the peroxidase (POD)-like activity. The absorbance of the color reaction (TMB at 652 nm) was recorded by a multi-mode reader within a certain reaction time to express the POD-like activity. In detail, 20 μL CDs solution (0.08 mg/mL), 30 μL H2O2 (1.2 M), and 20 μL TMB (0.4 mg/mL) were added into acetate buffer (pH 3.6), so as to display the chromogenic reaction and indicated POD-like activity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chemical titration of oxygen functional groups on CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To deactivate the ketonic carbonyl (CO), PH (50 μL, 1 mM) and HCl acid (5 μL, 38%) dissolved in ethanol were added into the prepared CDs (5 mL). The mixtures were then stirred for 36 h under N2 protection. Finally, a brown solution of the CDs-PH was obtained. Similarly, the reactions of hydroxy (C-OH), and carboxyl groups (OC-O) on the CDs were conducted using the titrants of BA and BrPE, respectively.

The CAT-like activity assays of CDs were carried out by measuring the generated oxygen at room temperature. 0.3 M H2O2 solution was added to buffer solution (pH 2.0, 5.0, 7.4 or 11.4) and then, 0.6 mg/mL CDs solution was added to the above mixture. The generated O2 solubility (unit: mg/L) was measured at different reaction time.

The SOD-like activity assays of CDs were carried out by ESR spectroscopy measurements. To verify the ability of CDs to scavenge superoxide anion (O2
−), xanthine and xanthine oxidase (XOD) were mixed in PBS buffer (pH 7.4) to generate O2
− and 5-tert-butoxycarbonyl 5-methyl-1-pyrroline-N-oxide (BMPO) was used to trap the superoxide in the form of spin adduct BMPO/
OOH.

Hydroxyl radicals (
OH) was generated by Fe2+/H2O2 system, which then reacted with a spin-trap reagent 5-tert-butoxycarbonyl 5-methyl-1-pyrroline-N-oxide (BMPO) to form BMPO/
OH adducts, and were detected by ESR spectroscopy. ESR signals were recorded after mixing 50 µM FeSO4 with 100 mM BMPO, 10 mM H2O2, and various concentrations of CDs.

RAW264.7 cells were seeded in 35 × 35 mm culture plates or 96 well plates, and then maintained for 24 h in RPMI containing 2% (v/v) fetal bovine serum (FBS) in a humidified incubator with 5% CO2 atmosphere at 37 °C. These cells were incubated with different concentrations of CDs for 24 h and LPS (1 μg/mL) for 24 h, respectively. ROS production levels were investigated under a DSU live-cell confocal microscope system or measured using a multimode reader with an excitation wavelength of 488 nm and an emission wavelength of 525 nm. The level of TNF-α in the cell supernatant was determined by an ELISA kit in accordance with the manufacturer's instructions (Beyotime, China).

To investigate the in vivo effectiveness of CDs for anti-inflammation, we established a mouse inflammatory model via caudal injection of Lipopolysaccharide (LPS). ICR mice (6–8 weeks old) were purchased from Chongqing Ensiweier Biotechnology Co., LTD. The mice were randomly assigned to 4 groups (with 5 mice in each group): normal group (Group 1, as control), LPS-treated group (Group 2), CDs-LPS-treated group (Group 3 and Group 4). In addition to the daily feeding of ordinary diet, mice in Group 3 were treated intravenously with 5 mg/kg body weight (BW)/3 day CDs for a week; while mice in Group 4 were intravenously injected with 20 mg/kg BW/3 day CDs for a week; mice in Groups 1 and 2 were treated with equivalent dose of normal saline though tail intravenous injection for a week. At last, mice in Groups 2, 3 and 4 were treated with 500 μg/kg LPS injecting to the caudal vein of mice, wherein the LPS induced oxidative stress and the inflammatory responses in the model mice.

The mice were killed 12 h later, and the liver tissues from all groups were harvested, weighed and divided into two halves. One half of their liver tissues were fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde, sectioned and embedded in paraffin, and then made into slices, routine hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) stained and morphology observed by microscope. The terminal deoxynucleotidyl-transferase mediated dUTP nick end labelling (TUNEL) method was used to evaluate apoptosis in liver tissue. The other half of liver tissue was homogenized with cold PBS to detect ROS levels. 0.5 g of liver tissue was taken from the control group and the treatment group, quickly removed and put into normal saline. The mixtures were homogenized by homogenizing machine, the homogenate was centrifuged at 3000 g at 4 °C for 30 min, and 5 μL of supernatant was diluted 100 times with a pipette, transferred to a 96-well plate (100 μL/well) and incubated for 5 min to rewarm to room temperature. 100 μL 20 μM DCFH-DA as added to each well and incubated at 37 °C for 30 min. At last, the fluorescence signal was measured using a multimode reader with an excitation wavelength of 488 nm and an emission wavelength of 525 nm.

The average size of the hydrothermally prepared CDs was 2.76 ± 1.2 nm and the distinct lattice was 0.23 nm, corresponding to the (100) crystal plane of graphitic carbon (Fig. 1 A) [40]. The synthesized CDs exhibited a clear peak at about 250 nm and a characteristic peak at 400 nm, respectively, which can be assigned to π-π* electronic transition of CC and n-π* electronic transition of the CO and CN ( Fig. S1
A) [41]. In Raman spectra, there was a typical G band at 1551 cm−1 ascribed to the sp2 carbon forms in the graphene structures, and a D peak at 1378 cm−1 related to sp3 carbons (
Fig S1
B) [42]. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy (Fig. 1B) showed that the -CN, -CC and -CO bands at 1416 cm−1, 1546 cm−1 and 1639 cm−1 existed in the CDs. The -OH and -NH bands at range of 3500–3250 cm−1 confirmed the existence of various hydrophilic functional groups on the surface of CDs, which benefited their biocompatibility [43]. The X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) indicated that CDs were mainly composed of carbon, oxygen, and nitrogen elements (Fig. 1C). High-resolution C 1 s consisted of four peaks at 284.1, 285.1, 285.9 and 287.5 eV, which can be assigned to -CC/CC, CN, CO- and -CO/CN, respectively (Fig. 1D). Three peaks for the high-resolution N 1 s revealed the coexistense of -C-NH-C- (398.3 eV), -CN (399.1 eV) and –NH2 (400.1 eV) (Fig. 1E). The O 1 s region consisted of three peaks at 531.1, 531.9, and 533.2 eV, which corresponded to -CO, OC-O-, and -C-OH groups, respectively (Fig. 1F) [44].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Multienzyme-like activities of CDs and ROS depletion
ce:section.ce:para:
Interestingly, the as-prepared CDs showed multiple enzymatic performances. Firstly, 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), an often used natural POD substrate was used to investigate the POD-like activity of CDs (Fig. 2 A, B). In the presense of CDs, deep blue solution appeared for H2O2 and TMB mixtures, as a significant contrary to the control experiments, in which negligible color change could be observed without CDs (Fig. 2A inset), demonstrating that the CDs had POD-like activity. The catalytic reaction was enhanced with increasing the CDs concentrations ( Fig. S2 ) and the CDs showed the best catalytic activity at pH 3.6 ( Fig. S3
A) with good recyclability ( Fig. S3
B).

In the steady-state kinetic assays, catalytic reactions were carried out under the optimized conditions with the various concentrations of the TMB or H2O2 substrates (pH 3.6). The Michaelis-Menten constant (K m) and maximal reaction velocity (v max) toward TMB as 2.51 mM and 1.67 × 10−7 M s−1, and toward H2O2 as 65.98 mM and 4.50 × 10−8 M s−1 were obtained by Michaelis − Menten curves ( Fig. S9 ). Then, the k cat and k cat/K m values of active CDs was calculated as 3.04 × 104 s−1 and 1.21 × 107 M−1 s−1, which was comparable with the natural enzyme HPR (Table S1) [46]. These data showed that TMB had strong affinity with CDs, which was likely attributed to electrostatic interaction owing to the negative and positive zeta potential of CDs and TMB ( Fig. S10 ) and the strong interaction between amino groups and CO groups, respectively. That is, TMB was absorbed on the surface of CDs, donating the lone-pair electrons of its amino groups to the sp2-hybrided carbon of the graphene structure, bridging the electron transfer from CDs to H2O2, successfully accelerating the oxidation process of the chromogenic substrate TMB (Fig. 2B) [47,48]. Under acidic conditions, the possible pathways of POD-like activity was described as following reactions (1) − (4):
(1) TMB − e → oxidized TMB (ox-TMB)
(2) CDs + e → CDs*
(3) CDs* + H2O2 + 2H+ + 2e → 2H2O + CDs
(4) TMB + H2O2 + 2H+ →CDs ox-TMB + 2H2O

Secondly, CAT-like property of the CDs was identified in the alkaline environment. Observable bubbles were observed in the H2O2 solution with CDs by naked eye at pH 7.4 and 11.4 as compared to those at pH 2.0 and 5.0 (Fig. 2C). There were much more bubbles produced at pH 11.4 than that at pH 7.4. These bubbles corresponding to the produced O2 identified by a dissolved oxygen electrode increased gradually with time and pH (Fig. 2D). The enhanced catalytic CAT-like activity at the high pH was attributed to the low redox potential of H2O2/O2 (0.695 V). In this situation, the CDs with OH bands formed anions, which can accelerate the electron transfer, making H2O2 more easily oxidized into O2 [49]. The reaction mainly undergoes three steps as equations (5) − (7): (i) the substitution reaction, i.e., HOO of H2O2 substitutes OH of CDs-OH− to form CDs-OOH−; (ii) CDs-OOH− captures one of H+ from another H2O2 to give CDs-O, H2O, and HOO−, and (iii) the interaction of CDs-O with HOO− to release O2 and CDs-OH−. The total reaction was H2O2 → 1/2O2 + 2H2O, in which CDs-OH− acts as a catalyst (equations (8)).
(5) CDs-OH− + H2O2 → CDs-OOH− + H2O
(6) CDs-OOH− + H2O2 → CDs-O + HOO− + H2O
(7) CDs-O + HOO− → CDs-OH− + O2
(8) H2O2 →CDs-OH− O2 + H2O

Thirdly, the SOD-like activity of the as-prepared CDs was valued by ESR analysis. As Fig. 2E showed, O2
− was effectively depleted under pH 7.4 by the as-prepared CDs, wherein, a spin trap reagent BMPO for O2
− was employed through the formation of BMPO/
OOH intermediate to produce ESR signal. The ESR signal was decreased with the increasing concentration of CDs, indicating the CDs exhibited SOD-like activity. It was noted that the CDs had low redox potential (0.38 V) ( Fig. S11 ) at pH 7.4, and thus were easily reduced by O2
− to H2O2 (O2
−/H2O2, 1.5 V) in the O2
− scavenging process. Meanwhile, the
OOH in the BMPO/
OOH intermediate was attracted on the surface of CDs that shared the lone-pair electrons with the sp2 carbon (CC), transferring O2
− to O2 (O2/O2
−, 0.73 V). In such case, the CDs-catalyzed scavenging of O2− had the same mechanism as SOD does (reaction (9) − (10)) [49]:
(9)
OOH + 2H+ + CDs → H2O2 + CDs*
(10) CDs* + 2
OOH → 2O2 + 2H+ + CDs

At last, hydroxyl radical-generating Fenton reaction of Fe2+/H2O2 was employed to porduce and test the
OH depleting ability of CDs in acidic environments. Without CDs, the Fe2+/H2O2 system exhibited their characteristic BMPO/
OH ESR signal peak. While with the addition of increasing concentration of CDs from 0 to 30 μg/mL, there was a steep decline for BMPO/
OH ESR signal intensity displayed. And no obvious BMPO/
OH signal was produced by the CDs themselves (Fig. 2F, S12). These phenomena convinced that CDs had a direct
OH scavenging capability, owing to the reduction capacity of CDs which had low redox potential (0.42 V) at pH 3.6 ( Fig. S13 ) as compared to
OH/H2O (2.9 V). The reduction of •OH by CDs was reflected through reactions (11) − (12):
(11) CDs + e → CDs*
(12) •OH − e + H+ → H2O

ce:section.ce:section-title: Anti-inflammatory effect of CDs in vitro and in vivo
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the anti-inflammatory ability of the CDs, RAW264.7 cells were treated with LPS (1 μg/mL) to establish a well-defined inflammatory model with relative high-level ROS and pro-inflammatory cytokines such as tumor necrosis factor (TNF-α) [54]. After 24 h, the cells showed significant morphological changes, including hypertrophy and extensive vacuolization (Fig. 3B, b, f, j). It was obviously that the inflammation of RAW264.7 macrophages were significantly suppressed with increasing CDs concentration (Fig. 3B, c − d, g − h, k − l), and the levels of the ROS and TNF-α were significantly reduced accordingly (Fig. 3C, S21). Fig. 3D showed that CDs were culminated in a dose-dependent decreased in the LPS-induced expression of proinflammatory mediator, including nitric oxide synthase (iNOS) and cyclooxygenase-2 (COX-2) [54]. Furthermore, the fluorescence intensity of DCF measured by a multimode reader was proportional to the intracellular ROS level, providing the possibility for detecting ROS in living cells (Fig. 3E, S22). These results indicated that CDs were able to deplete ROS and effectively inhibit inflammatory response in inflammatory cells.

Fig. 4
A showed the distribution of CDs in tail vein injected mice with 20 mg/kg CDs. Intense focal fluorescent signals in the liver were clearly displayed by the Ex vivo imaging and the CDs were accumulated much more in the liver in 24 h than in other tissues. The results demonstrated the accumulation of CDs in the liver was higher than in other major organs (Fig. 4 B). The feasibility for CDs to suppress the ROS in mice liver inflammation models were further identified. It showed that the CDs inhibited the LPS-induced high ROS levels by measuring the fluorescence in the liver to express its oxidative stress statue (Fig. 4 C). Meanwhile, LPS significantly caused hepatic congestion, as compared to that no obvious edema lesions or colour abnormalities was observed in liver tissue of the control group (Fig. 4 C, the corresponding mice liver tissues at the bottom). In the preincubated CDs groups, however, the congestion was distinctly decreased. As shown in the yellow arrow in the H&E staining image, after the LPS induction, the liver tissues of mice exhibited focal nuclear pyknosis and inflammatory cell infiltration, which demonstrated that the acute hepatitis in the mice was succesfully induced by LPS (Fig. 4 D, a − d) [23]. It can be seen that, compared with the control group, there were almost no histological changes in the liver after the pretreatment of the CDs for the mice. Moreover, the number of TUNEL-positive cells was markedly decreased in the CDs pretreated group compared with the LPS group (Fig. 4D e − h). These results showed that CDs were able to act as the efficient ROS scavengers to reduce the oxidative damage and help to inhibit the inflammation.

In conclusion, a type of CDs with good antioxidant catalytic activities was developed by a simple hydrothermal method. The CDs were demonstrated to act as the efficient ROS scavengers and to be a promising nanocatalytic medicine for the targeting treatment of inflammatory liver diseases. These findings can help to understand the mechanisms of CDs as an effective nanocatalytic medicine for controlling ROS-induced oxidative damage. However, there are some challenges that still remained for us to directly identify reaction intermediates and make clear the relationship with the catalytic activities and the complex surface functionalization modifications of CDs.

Bo Kong: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Validation, Writing – original draft. Tong Yang: Visualization. Feng Cheng: Investigation. Yan Qian: Investigation. Chunmei Li: Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Lei Zhan: . Yuanfang Li: Supervision. Hongyan Zou: Methodology, Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Data curation, Writing – review & editing. Chengzhi Huang: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Resources, Data curation, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC, Grant No. 21976144, 21535006, and 21705130), Chongqing Talents Program for Outstanding Scientists (Grant No.cstc2021ycjh-bgzxm0178) and Natural Science Foundation of Chongqing, China (Grant No. cstc2020jcyj-msxmX0873).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2021.12.107.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
